# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Paranormal horror movies recommendation

**Score:** 12
**Body:** Recently I’ve been watching a lot of horror movies that involves the paranormal side of things. I mean yes we all know the conjuring series, insidious, paranormal activities and many more.

What are 5 recommendations of horror movies in similar categories that would make me scared for days. 

Open to any suggestions 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Amityville Horror

The Changeling 

The Entity
2. Burnt Offerings
3. The Brood is pretty scary
4. Burnt Offerings, Poltergeist
5. The Entity (1982)
6. Innkeepers

The Shining

Doctor Sleep (directors cut)

Rose Red

Frighteners
7. Talk to Me
8. The Shining & Session 9
  - Session 9 is a hidden gem.
9. The Changeling


The Orphanage


The Shining


The Ring


Pulse

## 2. The L-Shaped Room (1962)

**Score:** 7
**Body:** Featuring Brock Peters and Leslie Caron

It's a really profound movie for its time. It's about a young and pregnant French woman who's poor and unmarried. The themes and highlights are timeless and hold up into this modern day-in-age.

If you''re a fan of old black-and-white movies and don't mind British productions, you may very well like this. You can see it free on YouTube.

### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Sounds interesting 🤔 I haven't seen it yet but might check it later. Thanks

## 3. Need a Disney plus movie to watch

**Score:** 1
**Body:** What should i watch on Disney plus tonight? I started paying for it again and wanna get use of it before I have to cancel again. It can be something I've seen before or something obscure. Thanks! 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Smart house,
Johnny Tsunami,
BRINK!
  - Going old school! Zenon?
2. The new Jonas brother’s Christmas movie.
3. *Up* or *Inside Out* would be my first recommendations.

Strong contender would be *Toy Story 3*, but if you haven't already (or need a refresher), watch the first two, then 3.

All three are really great, insightful storytelling.
4. Angels In The Outfield
5. Alright, some of my favorites:


Goofy Movie, always. 


The Black Cauldron is an underrated favorite. 


Meet the Robinsons. 


Luca


Muppet Treasure Island or Christmas Carol


I just saw Flight of the Navigator is on there. It's old and weird.
6. Encanto!! 🩷

also Groot mini series 

ahh i dk.. if Spiderman in the spider verse is on Disney+ ? 🤔 if so that is worth watching any day! 🎉
7. The Mandalorian

WandaVision

Agatha All Along

Ms. Marvel

Andor
8. Rogue One
9. Darby O'Gill and The Little People(1959)

## 4. lesbian stoner movies

**Score:** 0
**Body:** looking for movie stoner movie recommendations i can never find any female/girl centered stoner comedies. if you have some let me know any of your favorites. bonus points if they’re gay

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Bottoms (2023) is probably the closest I can think of, very funny film
  - Definitely Bottoms
2. Smiley Face (2007) is not a lesbian movie but it is a stoner movie with a woman lead
3. Drive-Away Dolls is the closest I can think of. Booksmart as well.
  - Came here to say Drive Away Dolls.

Also Honey Don't, the sort of sequel.
4. But I'm a Cheerleader
  - Not a stoner movie, but it has lesbians and I have watched it stoned. Very, very funny
5. I’m not sure if it’s quite what you’re looking for but the first thing this makes me think of is Booksmart.
6. Not a movie, but Broad City on Comedy Central fits, and it is hilarious.
7. Smiley Face (2007)

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Has There Ever Been an Objectively Great Actor Who Completely Fucked Their Career Than Mickey Rourke?

**Score:** 5175
**Body:** I struggle to think of another one who comes close.

Rourke came out swinging in the early 1980s’ with a series of edgy, offbeat roles in acclaimed films like Body Heat, Diner, and The Pope of Greenwich Village. Lots of actors have been called ‘the next Marlon Brando,’ but it was a more accurate for Rourke than it was for other: like Brando, he was handsome in a grittier, more unconventional way than most movie stars, and he often went for more daring roles than your traditional leading man.

He peaked in 1986-87, with 9 1/2 Weeks making him a box office draw and the double shot of Barfly and Angel Heart standing as his best performances yet.

This should’ve been the start of an incredible career, but it’s actually where the trouble starts: he develops an attitude, thinking he’s above playing the Hollywood game. He turns down meaty roles like Rain Man and Die Hard so he can do embarrassing passion projects like Johnny Handsome and Wild Orchid. He starts to become known as being difficult to work with.

In 1991, well past his marketable prime, he makes the decision to take up professional boxing, a truly baffling side project for an actor. He’s nowhere near as good a boxer as he is an actor, but he never got the hint and further alienated people from wanting to work for him. His films during this time - Harley Davidson and the Marlboro Man, White Sands, FTW - are forgettable and embarrassing compared to the films he made in his prime.

Then come the facial surgeries. I’d say mid-1995 is when the decline of his looks. He also starts taking steroids to the point where his voice loses the suave, smooth tone of his youth and drops about two whole octaves. By 1998 he is completely indiscernible as the guy from 9 1/2 Weeks. His looks severely limit the range of roles he can take and his attitude remains unchanged.

He spends most of the early 2000’s languishing in obscurity, doing made-for-tv and direct-to-video schlock. He gets a few meaty roles (Sin City, a great cameo with Nicholson in The Pledge) but nothing that changes the downward trajectory.

Then in 2008, he gets an unprecedented second chance with The Wrestler. Finally, a role that suited his current appearance and range. It’s a tour de force performance as good as any he turned out in his early career. It looked like the unthinkable had finally happened and Rourke was finally going to return to form.

Up to the Oscars, Rourke seemed to be making a genuine effort to rehabilitate his image and make good on the new opportunities afforded to him. He is charming, friendly, and even contrite in interviews. He even lays off the surgeries for a while and begins to vaguely look like his old self again. Even when he loses the Oscar to Penn, he does so diplomatically.

Then? He gets a big supporting role in Iron Man 2, and what happens? He has a not so great time making it and immediately falls back into the arrogant ‘above it all’ attitude, and resumes regularly fucking up his face with surgeries.

Nowadays he’s a complete joke and has completely fucked up his legacy. Future generations are only going to know him as a grotesque weirdo who made a few good films once. It’s a shame, because he could have been at the level of a DDL or Philip Seymour Hoffman. He could have been an undisputed great.

Anyone else you can think who royally fucked their career trajectory as spectacularly as he did? Closest I can think of is current day Johnny Depp.



### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Tom Sizemore (RIP) had an outstanding run of supporting roles in the 90's - Heat, Saving Private Ryan, Natural Born Killers, True Romance, Devil in a Blue Dress, Bringing Out the Dead, Strange Days, etc. Then meth, sex tapes, beating up Heidi Fleiss and other weird shit happened.
  - I just learned that Spielberg made him do drug tests on the set of Saving Private Ryan under threat of firing him and reshooting everything.
I’m glad he kept him in. Played an amazing part
2. Chevy Chase is a huge comedic talent. Was the biggest thing in comedy but apparently an absolute nightmare to work with.
  - Chevy Chase and Keanu Reeves are perfect mirrors of each other. One was an undisputed brilliant comic actor who was so personally unlikeable he phased out of the A-list because nobody wanted to work with him.

Keanu Reeves was generally considered a bad actor and often made fun of for roles like Bram Stoker's Dracula but was such a good guy people in the business found roles for him suited to his strengths to the point that he's arguably way more popular today than he was in the 90s. They don't make a lot of Die Hard imitators like Speed anymore. He could have easily fell into the hole that swallowed Jean-Claude Van Damme and Steven Seagal. But just being a good guy made people want to work with him and now he's John Wick.
    - Keanu's skill set is limited but he puts in the work for what he can do. Martial arts and wire work training for The Matrix films, firearms training for the John Wick films. 
3. You know, RDJ, but he had a comeback. When I saw Roark was cast as Whiplash, I thought Marvel was going to become a vehicle to rehabilitate actors that had become obscure.
  - Came here to post RDJ, people completely forget he was a tremendous fuckup. He went “home” one night and went to sleep in the bed of a neighbor’s kid. He couldn’t get a job anywhere and made less than almost everyone in Iron Man and was a pain to insure.
4. Cuba Gooding Jr. 

Staring in movies, won prestigious awards and then mostly B tier movies or worse.
  - I always felt that winning the Oscar for Jerry Maguire was the start of Cuba's downfall. He had Boyz in the Hood, Judgment Night, Gladiator, and Outbreak, then did Jerry Maguire.

Outside of Men of Honor and What Dreams May Come, he did absolute shitty movies after that. 

And then came Radio.
5. John Wilkes Booth really tanked his acting career.

## 2. Which character is considered a cool by everyone, but you consider him a loser?

**Score:** 440
**Body:** Tony Montana from Scarface. He looks cool. In reality, he's a loser who lost everything because of his greed and eventually died.

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Boba Fett.

They could write all these expanded universe lore and spinoff shows after the fact to show how "badass" he is, but nothing will erase the fact that he got taken out by a half-blind, groggy guy *on accident* and he screamed like a lil bitch all the way down the Sarlacc's gullet.
  - He wasn’t even a bad ass in his own show. Bad asses don’t need constant saving from “the help”.
2. While yeah I agree he’s a complete ass the whole movie and not at all a good dude, the reason he dies isn’t greed, it’s his morals and refusal to kill an innocent wife and kids
  - I always wondered why Montana had to do the hit himself when all he basically did was drive the car.

At that point he is Sosa's equal in terms of leadership. 
Why would he not have one of his guys drive the car ?
3. Walter White should be up there
4. Matthew McConaughey in Dazed and Confused.
  - This was my immediate first thought...that said....fantastic movie.  I watch it every Spring...I keep getting older...Summer vacation stays the same age...
5. the joker and/or Harley Quinn. they're insane murderers that stand for nothing.
6. Jordan Belford.

Total cretin that is glorified in the movie
7. Michael Corleone from the Godfather franchise. His attitude and callousness towards Kay and his family led to his own downfall.

## 3. I always forget what an amazing movie this is until I watch it again.

**Score:** 406
**Body:** smart, fast, interesting, stellar cast, great twists, great ending.

  
completely baffled why its not more popular.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Such a good cast.

## 4. Still LMFAO to this . [ hot shots 2 ]

**Score:** 207

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Legendary movie
  - Better than legendary — a fun movie!
2. Most impressive thing is the shape Sheen was in. Dude was shredded
  - Steroids it's still really common in the industry




What's more surprising hes a massive coke head and hasn't stroked out 
3. I showed my wife these movies recently, forgot how great they were. The speed and density of jokes is staggering!

## 5. A high concept fun sci-fi movie staring Sam Rockwell????

**Score:** 148
**Body:** Sign me the fuck up.

I don’t want to know a single thing more about it, I’m on board.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Moon?
  - Moon is excellent, and very much high concept.  Especially considering that the main cast was only a single actor and a voice.

But I would not really call it "fun".  In reality, it is about as "fun" as "The Idaho Transfer".

Trailer:

https://youtu.be/SmayyNLwr2I
4. Don’t panic

6. Sam Rockwell has me on board for anything, honestly
7. Humma Kavula!
8. They already did Hitchhiker's, tho

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. The Running Man: why would a unique director wilfully become generic?

**Score:** 204
**Body:** I watched The Running Man earlier in the week, and thought it was a perfectly functional, decent action-comedy. I also can’t remember the last time I’ve been more baffled by the existence of a film, to the point where it felt like a director was performing self-sabotage for us all to see. When I watch a bad film from the likes of Zack Snyder, I can still understand the intent behind the piece, and the fact that as a director, he is playing to his own sensibilities.

But The Running Man is the kind of disappointment that truly baffles me. Edgar Wright is a highly distinctive director, and unlike Wes Anderson, there has been no expression of fatigue from the audience, whatsoever. Despite this, Wright decided to forsake his own style. I appreciated Last Night in SoHo (even though it jumped the shark in the final act), because there, it still felt like Wright traded off his style to try something distinctive. But with The Running Man, it truly baffles me to no end why a director like Wright would, as a conscious decision, make a film that feels like a David Leitch B-side.

There are two moments in this film which show that, to no surprise, Wright is still capable of bringing his unique sensibilities to the film. One which involves bullets fired to the beat of a song, and another in which Glen Powell’s face moves in the same direction as his face on a TV behind him. Both moments were shown in the trailer, and are not indicative at all of the rest of the film. If Wright is still capable of creating such moments (I mean, why wouldn’t he be?) then why choose not to? Again, I would understand and support if he abandoned his traditional style to aspire to something (like what PTA did with One Battle After Another, or Spielberg with Schindler’s List), but this is not one of those instances. It is a talented director striving toward mediocrity.

EDIT: My [full review](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=WZulA36AdGU) of the movie!

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I think a big problem is that he made an unnecessary rod for his own back by sticking to the formula of the book too much.

The book is a depressing, dystopian tale of a desperate and sickly man who survives more often than not by being shuttled around and being hidden by others.

Wright wanted to make a zany action comedy about a tough, charismatic guy with anger issues, who fights his way out of trouble.

The problem is that he tried to map the latter onto the former, despite the fact it can't support it, and made little to no changes to accomodate it. It'd be like shooting The Shining as a comedy. But changing nothing fundamental about the story or structure. None of your beats are going to work.

Wright was simply a bad fit for the material. Even the 80s version (which has plenty of one-liners and satire) understands that the material doesn't work unless everyone involved treats the stakes as deathly serious.

But Wright (as much as I love him) seemingly can't make a film unless it's drenched in irony. Everyone is a cartoon character. And this works when the script and story are in perfect harmony with his vision. But when it's fighting tooth and nail against it, you get this. Neither fish nor fowl.

It's the equivalent of steak-flavoured ice cream. Two things that are great in isolation. But absolutely do not work together. Meaning the project was fundamentally misconceived from the start. It just couldn't pick a lane.
  - These were my thoughts too on watching it. It's a fascinating example of the challenges in adapting a novel to screen. People have long criticised the 1987 version for "being nothing like the book" but I've always felt it was brilliant in how it distilled the core ideas into something that worked much more cinematically for that time. Wright was so restrained in trying to please/impress King while also trying hard to please his audience. The two sensibilities cancel each other out for the most part, leading to a strangely moot, grey experience.  


But my biggest confusion is the ending. How could it follow the book so closely, but then botch the ending so absolutely terribly? It has no resonance or coherence, it's just flat-out awful. I'm aware he was still editing it up to the later half of October, he must have had some truly tortuous nights if that ending is the best they could put together. 
    - That was my partners take. That ending just killed everything that came before it. It never hit high points but it was a great representation of the core story. Until the end when it decided to become something it wasn’t.
2. He’s been wanting to adapt The Running Man for a while. So clearly his heart was in it at some point.

Unfortunately the movie just didn’t come together. You ask why a director would make the conscious decision to make a mediocre movie (assuming that’s what you meant by “David Leitch b-side” — unless you really liked Bullet Train) … I mean, they usually don’t? 

I think what may have happened is that he made the decision to tone down his usual style from the get-go, but because this was a big studio blockbuster, he was also pressured to make some changes. I have a strong suspicion the (horrible!) ending was the result of some last minute rewrites and reshoots but who knows. Maybe that was always the intent, and he was just misguided.
3. Filmmaking is really fucking hard.


And the attention to detail in his films is really a lot of work.


At some point, you just don't have the energy or the inclination to keep doing the same thing over and over just because that's what people expect.


That's without going into the business side of things, which can limit time and freedom.
  - When you’re dealing with a decent budget you have to answer to the money supply
    - There are directors with final cut but it looks like Edgar Wright may not be among them anymore.
4. 'unlike Wes Anderson, there has been no expression of fatigue from the audience, whatsoever'

Anderson has actually become a much more consistent performer, even if on a mid-tier level, than Wright. Wright is less productive than Anderson, and seems to have even lost his cult following.
5. Sometimes you do a film for the check or to establish a relationship for a film you want to do later. Sometimes the producers have final say and you just do it and move on. He will be 100% okay.
6. The script has its fair share of setups that get paid off and the action scenes are well staged with some clever camera moves, it doesn't feel like Wright's strengths were completely erased to me

When it comes to the characters, the pace, and especially the last 5 minutes of the movie though, it really struggles 

There's something about passion projects like this and like Del Toro's Frankenstein where the emotional core and energy of the film get lost along the way

## 2. The trial in The Devils (1971) has some of the best acting I’ve ever seen

**Score:** 44
**Body:** **Source:** [**https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=1mcGWvfGsGM**](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=1mcGWvfGsGM)

Guillermo Del Toro recently raved about this film in an interview and revealed that Warner has purposefully blocked a wide-release of what’s considered one of the best British films ever made.

Oliver Reed is standing trial and gives an amazing performance as Father Grandier. What’s even more striking is how influential this film has clearly been to many future films/directors.

• Name of the Rose (1986) - has a very similar aesthetic to this film. • Passion of the Christ - the “dark baby” (evil looking baby in a black blanket) is taken directly from this film’s climax. • Del Toro’s set design for Frankenstein was directly inspired by this film. (He called it a “future version of the past”)

The film even has a nightmarish quality. Several interiors have a plain white backdrop, and the characters are often juxtaposed wearing black robes/masks. It looks stunning, especially during the court scene.

I ended up watching the film and thought it was really good and ahead of its time. If you can somehow rent it digitally, it’s really well worth watching just for Reed’s performance alone.

### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. What an incredible movie. I went in thinking it would be shocking for the sake of shock value. How wrong was I. Every unhinged thing happening on screen makes perfect sense in the context of the story that's being told.

Loved Oliver Reed's performance. He goes from douchey playboy to unlikely hero in the latter half of the film which is clearly a salute to The Passion of Joan of Arc. The themes successfully blend sex, politics, and the church in seamless way. I think the implication that religious fervor has a sexual dimension really pissed a lot of people off. It's a massive shame that the complete cut of this masterpiece is still *de facto* banned worldwide.

A movie I thought of after watching was Beau Travail. A nice double feature on the intersection of desire and violence, with Galoup being the parallel of Sister Jeanne.
2. Do you have a source on Mel Gibson taking inspiration from *The Devils* for *The Passion of the Christ*? If true, that's one hell of a rabbit hole. I would've expected him to consider *The Devils* blasphemous. But considering his brand of Sedevantism and generally loopy worldview, maybe nothing is off the table.
3. Warners, who’ve owned the rights since the original release, has vowed to never put out the original cut of this film in the US. 

Great film! I was lucky enough to catch a screening of a UK print of the movie, shown here in the US.

## 3. Portrait of a Lady on Fire. Page 28 text.

**Score:** 15
**Body:** I was curious as to what the words on page 28 said...

"…quelque éclat, en moins d’une heure il en sortit une fleur rouge qui ressemblait à celle de la grenade. Cette fleur dure peu de temps, puisque les mêmes vents qui la font éclore, la font aussi tomber."

**" …with a certain brilliance, in less than an hour a red flower sprang from it, resembling that of the pomegranate. This flower lasts only a short time, for the same winds that make it blossom also make it fall."**

I thought it was poignant and most likely deliberate.

Great film.

EDIT: Upon further research, I've learned that the pomegranate has historically been used as a [symbol of love](https://ellehartford.com/love-symbols-pomegranate/?srsltid=AfmBOooL5c3SX3voBOqRXl4atBrRY5fjgGr4bA1zZv8a8X-LZrTMqy8R).

### Top 0 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)


## 4. What Have You Been Watching? (Week of (November 16, 2025)

**Score:** 6
**Body:**  Please don't downvote opinions. Only downvote comments that don't contribute anything. Check out the [WHYBW archives](https://www.reddit.com/r/TrueFilm/wiki/index#wiki_what_have_you_been_watching.3F).

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I watched the ff. in cinema.

* Linda Linda Linda - coming age film about a high school band who just want to perform. Not the most innovative but shocking well made. I hated Ghost Cat Anzu made by the same director but this was actually charming and truly a grower of a film. Recommended.  
* Showgirls - God, watching this on Cinema was a different vibe entirely. It is just a fuller experience where the comedy and the 'body' horror of it all peers even better. High Schlock to be honest. It made me really like it again. I oddly have an on and off again love for this film lol. 
* Running Man - I was expecting a decent film from Wright. Not the biggest fan of his but this is corny. It does not help that Powell was horrendously lacking of any levels. He is just angry guy XXX times. I really want to pull for it but it just lacks basic of storytelling. Gosh.
  - My wife and I love showgirls - recently got the 4k and it’s glorious. We laugh through most of it.
2. **Keeper - 7.4/10**

I don't usually like or respond to horror movies, but this one actually scared me. The ending is way too convoluted, and less explanation would have likely made this one better. Perkins' direction is top-notch, though. The guy just needs a great script. 

**Honey Don't - 6.2/10**

It was better than I thought it would be and fvcking gruesome. Two deaths in this one might be the harshest thing either of the Coen brothers has done. Sadly, it's lacking their humor with way too many plot threads disappearing in the end. 

**Oh, Canada - 6.3/10**

Could Paul Schrader's latest film be a masterpiece? Well, the first 45 minutes or so had me believing. The film decidedly took a nose-dive after that. Richard Gere isn't up to the task of playing Leonard Fife, a dying documentary filmmaker giving one last interview as a confession. He's still a great star and holds the scene, but there were so many moments when the film needed more. Jacob Elordi is fine as the younger version of Fife. But both performances hit less with a decision by Schrader to have Gere show up as the younger Fife in flashbacks. It's an interesting tactic that may have worked with a better script. 

**Materialist - 4/10**

A week later, and I still don't know what this film is trying to do. Is it a romantic comedy? A satire of a romantic comedy? A dramedy that lacks drama and humor? Whatever it is, I've talked enough about it.
3. [**WEEK # 254:**](https://tilbageidanmark.tumblr.com/tagged/movies)


"*If there is hope, it must lie in the proles*".

**ORWELL: 2+2=5**, my 5th "documentary" by anti-colonialist Haitian director Raoul Peck. It's an unapologetic examination of fascism, totalitarianism and authoritarianism as reflected in George Orwell's life and work.

1984, the novel, was initially misunderstood as being anti-communist, but actually it was always about the oppression of the masses by an imperialist ruling class. Extreme violence, genocide, torture and misery, the tools of the absolute tyrants, are presented in the modern killing fields everywhere, in Babi Yar, Afghanistan, Gaza, El Salvador, Myanmar, etc.

Orwell arrived at his critique of the British Empire, because as a young man he was trying to escape his own lower-middle class upbringing, so he enlisted as a policeman in Burma for 5 years. All his class distinctions and political understanding came out as he realised the empire's constant need to keep the poor in the third world starving and subservient. Depressing.

Also, Peck's first short, made while he was a student of the Berlin film school: **120 MINUTES STRANGE** (1982). A man comes back to a woman at the apartment. Meh.

🍿

More with Welsh stage-actor Damian Lewis [He is the one who narrated the 'Orwell 2+2=5' documentary above]:

**STEPHEN SONDHEIM'S OLD FRIENDS**, my 7th wonderful 'Greatest Hits' performance of the genius composer. The more I embed myself in his work, the more I adore it. This is a flamboyant big West End ensemble tribute, staged one year after his death in 2022. It featured a line-up of two dozens venerable British actors and singers (as well as Bernadette Peters, naturally). Among the highlights: Bob Brydon, Judi Drench, Siân Phillip, Imelda Staunton, (new-to-me) [Janie Dee(!)](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=x9JMliCr4z4), and 92-yo Petula Clark, who slayed it singing ['I'm still here'.](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=N-DkyuvKjzQ) *[TIL that she has had the longest career of any British entertainer, spanning more than 80 years!]*. Too bad that the YouTube copy was of such a poor quality!

What a concert! With so many moving, enjoyable songs. Here is another cover of his [Not getting married.](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zRsdEcybJL0) And the [Madelaine Khan version.](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PrRDrz53Q1E)

I still have to discover some of Sondheim's most acclaimed plays, 'A little night music', 'Follies', 'Gypsy', 'Sweeney Todd', 'On the way to the forum', 'Dick Tracy', Etc... And I can't wait, after seeing all of them, to start re-watching them all, a second and third times. This is actually one of the few rays of sunshine I'm looking forward to in this near future of otherwise miserable and depressing prospects.

Every time I watch one of his shows, I decide to re-visit Noah Baumbach's 'A marriage Story', even though Adam Driver singing 'Feeling Alive' was only a 3 minute scene. Maybe next week...

🍿

More of Janie Dee!

“*Please just put in it!...*" In the 2016 short **THE DRIVER'S SEAT**, she and her middle-age husband are trying to have sex in the front seat of their little car.

🍿

"*In the Soviet Union they never eat!*"...

**MALENA** (2000), my 4th film by Giuseppe Tornatore, and like his 'Cinema Paradiso', it's a throwback to old time, small town Sicily. In this closed-minded, gossipy town, war widow Monica Bellucci is so voluptuous and so reclusive, that all the men desire her, and all the women are jealous of her. She has to be punished for her beauty, and the sensuous plot turns eventually sad. There's also a 13-yo boy who's so transfixed by her, that he can't stop jerking off.

The best parts: The nostalgic feels, played so many times before, of sun-drenched Italy in the 40's, and [Ennio Morricone's sentimental score](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=W-YD2Y8ojYE).

🍿

"*Revolutionary violence is the only way.*"

I worried that PT Anderson's heralded new **ONE BATTLE AFTER ANOTHER** would be a complex Pynchon'ian head trip, but actually it was a straight up, simple action thriller, played against the landscape of the American ICE Gestapo of 2025. [*"American Gestapo" will be a good name for a movie*]. Tense and entertaining, there was no doubt on which side of the political arena it plants its flag.

Armed resistance by a 'Weather Underground' type former revolutionary, who in retirement is just a paranoid stoner living off the grid, and protective of his 16-yo daughter. The overwhelming "Macht" of the fascist state is represented by cruel villain soldier Lockjaw Sean Penn, and even more chilling by the racist Secret Society cabal of Santa-worshipers, wealthy white supremacists, who "control everything". It's a scary, repressive vision of today's America, but at the center of the nearly 3-hour nightmarish thrill-ride is the warm love between the daughter and dad.

I liked the song [Perfidia](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=1j70E5ZR8S8) played at the end, and I'm sure I'll be watching this a few more times soon. [As well as 'Civil War'].

🍿

"*A girl and a gun...*"

After seeing 437 French films in the last 5 years [including 16 by Jean-Luc Godard, whom I'm not a fan of] and because generally I dislike fawning biopics of 'Important Artists', I was ready to hate Richard Linklater's new, French-speaking homage **NOUVELLE VAGUE**, about the 1960 shooting of "À bout de souffle/Breathless". It sounded like a wet dream fetish project by a pretentious hipster-cineaste.

**Surprise!** Within 10 minutes of meeting (Bad!) impressions of all my heroes from that period, from Suzanne Schiffman and Raoul Coutard to Agnès Varda and Claude Chabrol, I fell in love with this breezy, nostalgic re-enactment, and the smile did not leave my face until the end.

It wasn't deep, but it was **my most enjoyable film of the week!**

It opened with [Dalida singing Come Prima](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=AJGcmHf8b8Q), with the rest of the score a mellow Ben Webster'y jazzy mood.

The [Now Trailer](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0xWuVI9ctvU). The [original 1960 trailer](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Q_LuA0j0rfg). The [re-mastered, new trailer of the original](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=iAuqDI4EAl4). Now I'll look forward to seeing Linklater's other biopic 'Blue Moon'. *I should also re-watch the original 'À bout de souffle'.*

*(Continued below)*
  - *(Continued)*

**CAUGHT STEALING**, Darren Aronofsky's new black comedy. An excellent action flick about a scuzzy-looking deadbeat who gets inadvertently involved with the Russian mafia of 1998 Lower East Side. It includes two murderous Hasidim mobsters who drive a Previa van, as well as cameos of Laura Dern and Carol Kane.

🍿

"*Gregor is in a town called Arles...*"

Another slick action thriller, but one that didn't age well in the 27 years since it was made, John Frankenheimer's standard cookie-cutter **RONIN**. Most Hollywood movies from the 1990's [(with few exceptions)](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=q0tUj2_eTn0) did not stay fresh and cliche-free, and did all the car chase scenes then had to include one 'frenetic driving against incoming traffic on the freeway'?

3/10 is too generous.

🍿

**FRANCIS FORD COPPOLA X 2:**

🍿 **MEGADOC**, a new, interesting fly-on-the-wall documentary about a "Genius At Work"; the shooting of 'Megalopolis', 83-yo Coppola's epic last film. I saw this huge-budget Opus Magnum a little over one year ago, and it left no residue in my memory.

Still it was fascinating to observe the follies of an old, celebrated artist going for broke as he attempt to create another masterpiece, inventing megalomaniac characters who are delusional, Nietzschean and demented, risking his life long fortune on a vision that only he can see. Did it make me want to watch it again? Maybe not. But I wish there was a record like this about 'The Conversation', or 'The Godfather'.

Notes:

1. Giancarlo Esposito was born In Copenhagen.

2. [Cannibal murderer Shia LaBeouf](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=o0u4M6vppCI) is always a mistake.


🍿 Coppola created 5 enduring classics, but they were all done decades ago. The rest of his output is actually middling. Sitting through **RUMBLE FISH** (1983) for the first time was frustrating. A artful black and white film (but with a single patch of color, just like Kurosawa's 'High and low'), a remake of the 'rebel without a cause' trop, a fetishazation of the drift-less Bad Boy myth, it was full of macho and bravado, as practiced by teenager boys. Old time Tulsa with its empty night streets and neglected locations.

The gritty angst didn't work for me, but they were all so young; Mickey Rourke, Chris Penn, Tom Waits and Diane Lane. Even Tracey Walter got a cameo.

🍿

**PROBLEMISTA** (2023) is a terribly-surrealist indie production from A24 that got great reviews. A young, timid, twink creative from El Salvador is earnestly trying to start a career as a toy designer for Hasbro, before his work visa expires. While employed for a NYC company that cryogenically freezes dead artists, he meets insane lady Tilda Swinton, and somehow they develop a strange relationship. It's quirky, juvenile and charmless.

I must stop watch things just because they got high ratings. [2/10.](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=n0UFgbGZk10) 

🍿

**2 FIRST-WATCH EARLY CHAPLIN'S:**

🍿 **TILLIE'S PUNCTURED ROMANCE** (1914) was a Mack Sennett comedy, the first ever comedy feature, and the last Chaplin vehicle that he didn't write or directed himself. Actually he played only a feature actor here, (together with [Mabel Normand)](https://davidhudson.tumblr.com/post/799748251351973888/mabel-normand-november-9-1892-february-23). The headliner was the matron-type Marie Dressler, in a story where the negative Chaplin character is an unscrupulous and immoral playboy. Lots of literal butt kicking but not too good or too funny.

🍿 **THE PILGRIM** (1923), his last shorter-than-a feature (46 min.) minor work, about an escaped convict who steals a minister's clothes, and who has to play a pastor in the small town of Devil's Gulch, Texas.

There were only two memorable scenes: A rolling pin which was left on the top shelf that kept rolling down on everybody's heads, and a pantomime sermon of 'David and Goliath'.

🍿

"*I'm sorry about the balls!...*"

Because of [this gif](https://jesusismyhostage.tumblr.com/post/799696640125566976/ultrakillblast-in-the-mouth-of-madness-1995), I watched John Carpenter's **IN THE MOUTH OF MADNESS** even though I should have known better. 1994 Hollywood Pulp Horror mindset was bullshit, and this was like a stupid Stephen King, but even dumber. Insurance Investigator Sam Neill was no Walter Neff, and this was no 'Double Indemnity'. It was just terrible from start to finish. 1/10.

Also, **CAPTAIN VOYEUR**, Carpenter's first short from his time at USC Cinema in 1969. An early 'Halloween' concept about a masked peeper. 100% Amateur Hour - even worst than 'Mouth of Madness' if it can be imagined. 1/10.

🍿

**ONE WEEK AND A DAY** (2016) is nearly-good Israeli tragicomedy about mourning and marijuana. A couple of grief-stricken parents are having a hard time at the end of the "Shiva", coping with the death of their son. The pragmatic mother is anxious to return to her work as a teacher. The erratic and rude father meanwhile hangs with their slacker neighbor, smoking weed for the first time in his life, getting into all kind of low-key troubles and is emotionally adrift.

There are two excellent scenes that stood out: When the young stoner is putting heart and soul into a comic air-guitar performance, and a eulogy given by another mourner at another funeral, which is not part of this story, but gives it depth and meaning. 5/10.

🍿

**THE SHORTS:**

🍿 **I'M LOST IN THE HAZE**, a new surrealist Mid-journey short by one Kelly Boesch. Salvador Dali's eggs, eyeballs and spheres, well made and promising. I actually can't wait until a "real", "serious" filmmaker will embrace this blursed technology and create a 500 million dollar-looking epic film for $50,000 - probably in the next 12 months?... Calling Terry Gilliam?... [*Female Director*].

🍿 **1970: A BOY LAST SUMMER OF FREEDOM** is an episode of a long-lasting BBC documentary series about life on the islands. This is a lovely portrait of a cute 5-yo Scottish boy who enjoys an idyllic existence in a small Whiskey-producing village.

🍿

([**ALL MY FILM REVIEWS - HERE**](https://tilbageidanmark.tumblr.com/tagged/movies)).
4. I started off the week with a *Braveheart* and *The Patriot* for an episode of my [podcast](https://open.spotify.com/show/2oJdlITi9BNuIA3BCX0p65?si=DBbV07NkRlK73B8b44KmcA) which pits double features against one another and examines their similarities through a critical lens. Surpringly (?), Jason Isaacs was the standout in both features, based purely on the way he relishes his exaggerated villainy. It’s a total sanitizing of actual WWII history to equate his British Dragoon character with Nazi-SS, but it makes for some compulsively watchable cheese.

Then I watched two 1939 classics for the first time, *Only Angels Have Wings* and *The Rules of the Game*. Both excellent! A welcome change of pace from the week’s earlier faire. 

Over the following days, I watched *The Alien Report* (variously described as a 2023, 2024, and 2025 release) — a z-grade, vlog-style descent into psychosis of a Chicago man documenting his frequent abductions and run-ins with extraterrestrial that are monitoring him.

Afterward, I caught a showing of *The Passion of Joan of Arc* at my local arthouse, complete with choir accompaniment. This was my second time seeing the picture; unfortunately, the theater played a DVD of the film, which baffled me. Still, an amazing film. One of my favorites from the era and a testament to staying the course.

Last night, I watched *American Movie*. I found it equal parts charming and sad. I’ve seen some allege the film comes across as exploitation, although I didn’t really get this impression. It’s honest, even if you get the feeling the subject is playing up his megalomania to the camera.

A varied week!
  - Forgive me if Im wrong, but I think you meant Dragonheart. Solid film!
5. **The Chaperone** (2018) \*\* Based on a book by a writer from KU, this tells the "true story" of when 15-year-old Louise Brooks first moved to New York to become a dancer accompanied by an adult chaperone, played by Elizabeth McGovern. The story is more about the chaperone and there isn't much of historical interest. It's just another dull Masterpiece Theatre period production. 

**Soldier of Orange** (1977) \*\*\*\* Similar to Verhoeven's *Black Book*, this is a sprawling WWII film about the Dutch resistance following several college friends that pull each other into defying Nazi rule. It covers a lot of ground over 2h45m, but thanks to Verhoeven's brisk, assured pacing, it never gets dull. While every character gets their story told, the main focus is on Ruger Hauer, who is simply fantastic. It's a shame he wasn't in more Verhoeven films in the U.S. *IDMB: In 2010, this movie was adapted into a musical, which became the most successful and long-running musical in Dutch history.*

**Out of the Clouds** (1955) \*\*\* This is an odd movie. It's basically a soap opera set in an airport, and we spend as much time learning how airports operate as we do with romances and personal dramas. 75 years later, all the vintage airport stuff is far more interesting. 

**Back to God's Country** (1919) \*\*\* After watching Nell Shipman drive over mountains in *Something New*, I wanted more of this adventurous Canadian outdoorswoman. Here she's a nature-loving Snow White character surrounded by friendly animals who meets up with a lustful, evil baddie posing as a Mountie. The story is a more conventional melodrama, and like *Something New*, it ends with a massive chase, this time on dogsleds through the snow, which isn't nearly as impressive. I appreciate that in both films she's saving a man in distress. So progressive. 

**The Grubstake Remix** (2015) \* I've toyed with the idea of taking a silent film and removing the titles and adding dialogue and sound effects. *The Grubstake Remix* is a group doing this live using a Nell Shipman movie from 1923. Unfortunately, they decided to use Shakespeare for all the dialogue, which not only has nothing to do with the movie, it just makes it really hard to follow, and there's no attempt to match it up with lip movement. An experiment gone wrong. I couldn't finish it. I'll have to hunt down the original version. 
6. **Best to worst**

**Annie Hall (1977) – 10/10**  
Fourth rewatch. Since it’s a film I revisit every couple of years, it’s fascinating how my perception shifts while the rating stays the same. This time I didn’t see the romance as something grand or once-in-a-lifetime. More like two people who aren’t particularly well-matched, but who still give each other something valuable before parting ways.

**Rope (1948) – 9/10**  
Hitchcock simply blew his contemporaries out of the water. The cinematography is absurdly good – it’s as if he decided to make an entire film look like the single best shot from **Notorious** (the famous one, with the key), only in Technicolor. What a guy.

**Double Indemnity (1944) – 9/10**  
Third rewatch. An almost perfect movie. MacMurray is fine, but he feels rather bleak next to the supernova of charisma that is Barbara Stanwyck. I’m pretty sure that if someone like Bogart had played his role, the film’s status might rival **Casablanca**.

**Arsenic and Old Lace (1944) – 8/10**  
Third rewatch. Funny how even Grant struggles a bit with some of the over-the-top moments. The film is incredibly bold, really; if I were a director, I’d hesitate to walk onto set with a script *this* goofy. But Capra pulls it off somehow… at least most of the time.

**Germania Anno Zero (1948) – 8/10**  
Easily the strongest entry in Rossellini’s war trilogy. Also fascinating due to historical context.

**The Heiress (1949) – 8/10**  
Great writing and strong performances. It’s always nice to see such an intelligently crafted, female-driven story from that era. The ending could have landed with more force, but that’s really my only complaint.

**Spring in a Small Town (1948) – 7/10**  
A structure almost too typical of Asian melodrama: a woman torn between desire and marital duty. Same story beats whether in China, India, or Japan. Very solid overall, even if a bit dry.

**Nora Inu (1949) – 6.5/10**  
Not much to say. Kurosawa’s noir from the previous year (**Drunken Angel**) was much better. The protagonist’s obsession with retrieving his gun is hard to relate to, and his guilt feels unconvincing as a narrative driver. One character even tells him outright that “the killer would’ve killed regardless, gun or no gun.” Why so serious, Mr. Mifune? There’s no deeper motive under the surface, too.

**Battleground (1949) – 6.5/10**  
I’ve never been a fan of American post-war depictions of WWII, and **Battleground** is no exception. To be fair, it does move in the right direction; that is, portraying war as more than a dangerous adventure and emphasizing its mud-soaked mundanity. But the writing is too silly and the performances too mediocre to push my rating higher. Fun fact: one character looks  like Hasan Piker, and his name in the film is Hansan. Lol.

**The Fountainhead (1949) – 5.5/10**  
Incredibly strange across the board. The script, written by Ayn Rand, is a bizarre blend of comical preachiness and an intriguing premise (brutalist but triumphant). There is genuine chemistry between Cooper and Neal, and a lot of cardboard supporting characters. Cooper carries it hard tho.

**All the King’s Men (1949) – 5/10**  
Watching this slog without coffee was a challenge. It’s like **Citizen Kane** stripped of everything that made **Citizen Kane** good.

## 5. I don't get Sam Peckinpah

**Score:** 1
**Body:** This isn't a ragebait post or anything, I'm just curious to hear some different perspectives on peckinpah's films. A lot of directors I respect praise him highly, but the films I've seen all left me sort of dissatisfied. So far I've seen The Wild Bunch, Straw Dogs, Bring me the Head of Alfredo Garcia and Pat Garrett and Billy the Kid, The last of which I actually adored, but it has a more light hearted/poetic tone than the other films which I found to edge on misery porn. Perhaps it's the raw ugliness fans of his seem to enjoy that I find often times revolting.

What do you see in his films, if you're a Peckinpah fan? Which of his films do you enjoy most? I think I'll see Cross of Iron next and maybe The Getaway just so I know I've given his movies an honest chance.

### Top 2 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I've only seen two of his movies, Straw Dogs and The Wild Bunch. I didn't like The Wild Bunch (probably need to watch it again, I was maybe too young at the time to appreciate it), but Straw Dogs was great. It feels like a fable about masculine insecurity and paranoia. If it's read literally, I can understand how offensive the scene of the woman starting to enjoy rape is, but I didn't view it literally. It's just a projection of a man's worst fears, and the way women become objects or prizes to be won or lost (or taken by force) in men's fantasies and nightmares.
2. Perhaps you can lead this discussion by listing the high praise from a lot of directors and how you disagree with their comments?

I can't imagine they all simply boil Peckinpah down to "raw ugliness" and praise that, so what are you actually referring to?

Isn't your curiosity satisfied by those directors, and their different perspectives on Peckinpah's various films?

How would reddit folks be able to show you the way when the directors you respect can't help you figure out what you're not getting?
  - "People have said" ...  
"Why do people say" ...  
"They are saying" ...

Who needs a source? Just reference a vague group of people who say vague things.

Then argue against the vague proposition and give a shrug, "Can somebody explain this to me?"

Voila! Instant Reddit post!
  - Maybe get a reading comprehension test, the post asks what elements you enjoy about peckinpah's movies. I'm not looking to have you explain to me how to enjoy the movies as you seem to be implying. 

Next time you respond to a post, imagine you're on a date, and imagine the pussy you won't be getting if you say the thing you want to say.
    - You should watch Straw Dogs again.
